items.q: What is the denary value of binary 10110?
items.why: Students forget to apply place values. Each position is a power of 2 from right (1, 2, 4, 8, 16...). Reading binary as denary is the most common error.

items.q: What is the difference between an IP address and a MAC address?
items.correct: MAC is a hardware identifier (fixed); IP is a logical network address (assignable).
items.misconception: They are the same thing with different names.
items.random_wrong: IP is for input, MAC is for output.
items.why: Students treat these as synonyms. MAC is burned into the network card (Layer 2); IP is assigned by the network (Layer 3). Both are needed to route packets to a device.

items.q: Why are passwords stored as hashes rather than plaintext?
items.correct: Hashes are one-way - even an attacker who steals them cannot easily recover passwords.
items.misconception: Hashes are encryption that can be decrypted with the right key.
items.random_wrong: Hashes compress the data to save space.
items.why: Students confuse hashing with encryption. Hashes have NO reverse function - you can verify a password by re-hashing, but cannot decrypt the stored hash to get the original.
The answer examiners want is: Microsoft Word. “Linux kernel” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's Has visible source code. “Always free of charge” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many main categories of software are taught at GCSE (system and application)?
items.why: Exact answer: 2 categories. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
